content.text: (also called Points of Presence, or PoPs) are network facilities located in cities around the world, distinct from AWS Regions. They are not general-purpose compute environments — they run a focused set of services that require the lowest possible latency to end users: CloudFront, Route 53, AWS Shield, and AWS WAF. With 600+ Edge Locations in 90+ cities as of 2025, AWS can serve most internet users from a PoP within 20–30 ms.

attrs.alt: Three-tier AWS delivery hierarchy: Edge Locations closest to users, Regional Edge Caches in the middle, AWS Regions at origin

content.text: Edge Locations vs. Regions vs. AZs
These three infrastructure tiers serve fundamentally different purposes:
a place to deploy EC2 instances or RDS databases. They are caching and routing infrastructure managed entirely by AWS. For compute close to users, see
Four core AWS services use Edge Locations to reduce latency and absorb traffic close to users:
CloudFront is the primary consumer of Edge Locations. A CloudFront
has an origin (S3, ALB, EC2, custom HTTP server) and caches responses at Edge Locations nearest to each requesting user. The cache hierarchy has three tiers:
: First-hit cache. 600+ worldwide. If the object is cached here, it is served in milliseconds with no origin call.
: Intermediate cache with 12× the storage of individual PoPs. If the PoP misses, the REC is checked before going to origin. There are 13 RECs globally.
: The authoritative source (S3 bucket, ALB endpoint, etc.) in an AWS Region. Hit only when both PoP and REC miss.

Route 53 name servers are distributed across Edge Locations. When a user queries your domain, the DNS query is handled by the nearest Route 53 PoP, reducing DNS resolution latency to under 10 ms in most regions. Route 53 also uses Edge Locations for:
: Route 53 health-check probes originate from Edge Locations in multiple regions simultaneously, detecting endpoint failures within seconds.
: Measures which Region is closest to the DNS client by using the latency from that client's Edge Location to each Region.
: Resolved at the Edge Location, which identifies the approximate location of the DNS query.
AWS Shield Standard is automatically applied to all Edge Locations at no charge. It absorbs large volumetric DDoS attacks (UDP floods, SYN floods, reflection attacks)
, before traffic ever reaches your origin. Shield Advanced extends this with 24/7 DDoS response team access and cost protection.
AWS WAF runs at the same Edge Locations as CloudFront. Web ACLs inspect HTTP requests
, blocking SQL injection, XSS, and custom patterns in under 1 ms of added latency:

# Create a WAF Web ACL attached to CloudFront (runs at all Edge Locations)
# Note: CloudFront WAF must be created in us-east-1 (global)
WEB_ACL_ARN=$(aws wafv2 create-web-acl \
  --name prod-edge-waf \
  --scope CLOUDFRONT \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --default-action Allow={} \
  --rules '[
    {
      "Name": "AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet",
      "Priority": 1,
      "OverrideAction": {"None": {}},
      "Statement": {
        "ManagedRuleGroupStatement": {
          "VendorName": "AWS",
          "Name": "AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet"
        }
      },
      "VisibilityConfig": {
        "SampledRequestsEnabled": true,
        "CloudWatchMetricsEnabled": true,
        "MetricName": "CommonRuleSetMetric"
      }
    }
  ]' \
  --visibility-config SampledRequestsEnabled=true,CloudWatchMetricsEnabled=true,MetricName=ProdEdgeWAF \
  --query 'Summary.ARN' --output text)

echo "WAF Web ACL: $WEB_ACL_ARN"

# Attach to CloudFront distribution
aws cloudfront update-distribution \
  --id "$DIST_ID" \
  --distribution-config "$(aws cloudfront get-distribution-config \
    --id "$DIST_ID" --query 'DistributionConfig' --output json | \
    jq '.WebACLId = "'$WEB_ACL_ARN'"')" \
  --if-match "$(aws cloudfront get-distribution-config \
    --id "$DIST_ID" --query 'ETag' --output text)" \
  --query 'Distribution.Status' --output text
, eliminating the latency of a round-trip to an AWS Region for dynamic request handling:
Per invocation only (10× cheaper than L@E)
A/B testing, auth, dynamic routing, image resize

# Lambda@Edge: origin request — resize images on the fly before caching at the PoP
# Triggered when CloudFront makes a request to the S3 origin (runs at origin-side PoP)
import json
import re

def handler(event, context):
    request = event['Records'][0]['cf']['request']
    uri = request['uri']

    # Parse /images/photo.jpg?w=300&h=200 → /images/300x200/photo.jpg
    qs = request.get('querystring', '')
    params = dict(p.split('=') for p in qs.split('&') if '=' in p)
    w = params.get('w', '')
    h = params.get('h', '')

    if w and h and re.match(r'^/images/', uri):
        # Rewrite URI to a pre-rendered size variant stored in S3
        base = uri.replace('/images/', '')
        request['uri'] = f'/images/{w}x{h}/{base}'
        request['querystring'] = ''

    return request
Price Classes: Controlling Which PoPs Serve Your Content
CloudFront lets you restrict which Edge Locations serve your distribution via
. Choosing a smaller set of PoPs reduces cost but increases latency for users outside the selected regions:
US, Canada, Europe, Asia, Middle East, Africa
Primarily US/EU/Asia audience; excludes South America
US/EU-only audience; cheapest option; high latency for APAC/LATAM

# Update a distribution to use PriceClass_100 (US + Europe only)
CONFIG=$(aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id "$DIST_ID")
ETAG=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.ETag')

echo "$CONFIG" | jq '.DistributionConfig.PriceClass = "PriceClass_100"' | \
  jq '.DistributionConfig' > /tmp/updated-config.json

aws cloudfront update-distribution \
  --id "$DIST_ID" \
  --distribution-config file:///tmp/updated-config.json \
  --if-match "$ETAG" \
  --query 'Distribution.{Status:Status,PriceClass:DistributionConfig.PriceClass}'
Invalidating Cached Content at Edge Locations
When you update content at the origin, cached copies at Edge Locations serve stale data until the TTL expires.
force PoPs to discard cached objects immediately. Each invalidation costs $0.005 per path after the first 1,000 per month:

# Invalidate specific paths at all Edge Locations (wildcard supported)
INVALIDATION_ID=$(aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id "$DIST_ID" \
  --paths '/images/*' '/api/v1/config.json' '/index.html' \
  --query 'Invalidation.Id' --output text)

echo "Invalidation: $INVALIDATION_ID"

# Wait for propagation to all PoPs (typically 10-60 seconds)
aws cloudfront wait invalidation-completed \
  --distribution-id "$DIST_ID" \
  --id "$INVALIDATION_ID"
echo "Invalidation complete — all PoPs updated"

# Better alternative: use versioned URLs and long TTLs
# /static/app.v2.3.1.js (never invalidate — let TTL of 1 year run)
# /index.html (short TTL of 60s or no-cache; references versioned assets)
CloudFront publishes metrics to CloudWatch that let you measure cache hit ratios, latency, and error rates per Edge Location. The
is the most important operational metric — a low ratio means most requests reach your origin, defeating the purpose of the CDN:

# Check CloudFront cache hit rate (should be > 80% for static content)
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/CloudFront \
  --metric-name CacheHitRate \
  --dimensions Name=DistributionId,Value="$DIST_ID" Name=Region,Value=Global \
  --start-time "$(date -u -d '1 hour ago' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')" \
  --end-time "$(date -u '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')" \
  --period 300 \
  --statistics Average \
  --query 'Datapoints[*].{Time:Timestamp,CacheHitRate:Average}' \
  --output table

# CloudFront real-time logs: stream per-request data to Kinesis for analysis
aws cloudfront create-realtime-log-config \
  --end-points '[{
    "StreamType": "Kinesis",
    "KinesisStreamConfig": {
      "RoleARN": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CloudFrontRealtimeLogs",
      "StreamARN": "arn:aws:kinesis:us-east-1:123456789012:stream/cf-logs"
    }
  }]' \
  --fields 'timestamp' 'c-ip' 'cs-uri-stem' 'sc-status' 'x-edge-location' \
           'x-edge-result-type' 'time-taken' 'cs-referer' \
  --sampling-rate 100 \
  --name prod-realtime-logs \
  --query 'RealtimeLogConfig.ARN' --output text
Edge Locations are optimized for a specific workload profile. Understanding their limits prevents misuse:
: CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge are stateless. Persistent data (databases, user sessions) must live in an AWS Region.
: Only CloudFront, Route 53, Shield, WAF, and the two compute primitives run at Edge Locations. EC2, RDS, and most other services require a Region.
: After deploying a new Lambda@Edge version, replication to all PoPs takes 1–5 minutes. Plan deployments accordingly.
No VPC access for CloudFront Functions
: CloudFront Functions run in a shared execution environment and cannot reach private VPC resources. Lambda@Edge can reach VPC resources via the origin request hook.
: After the first 1,000 invalidation paths per month, each path costs $0.005. High-frequency content updates should use versioned URLs instead.
: In opt-in Regions (e.g., ap-east-1, me-south-1), Edge Location availability and service support may be limited. Check the CloudFront feature availability matrix.
Between Regional Edge Caches and the origin, CloudFront offers an optional fourth caching tier called
. It collapses all cache-miss traffic from every Regional Edge Cache into a single origin region, reducing the number of simultaneous origin requests from
Origin Shield is most valuable for:
: APIs or personalised content where many requests bypass Regional Edge Caches.
: On-premise servers or underpowered EC2 instances that cannot handle spikes from multiple RECs simultaneously.
: Origin in ap-northeast-1 but users worldwide — Origin Shield in ap-northeast-1 absorbs all inbound misses before they hit the origin.

# Enable Origin Shield on a CloudFront distribution origin
# Specify the AWS Region closest to your origin server
CONFIG=$(aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id "$DIST_ID")
ETAG=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.ETag')

# Add OriginShield to the origin configuration
echo "$CONFIG" | jq '
  .DistributionConfig.Origins.Items[0].OriginShield = {
    "Enabled": true,
    "OriginShieldRegion": "us-east-1"
  }
' | jq '.DistributionConfig' > /tmp/origin-shield-config.json

aws cloudfront update-distribution \
  --id "$DIST_ID" \
  --distribution-config file:///tmp/origin-shield-config.json \
  --if-match "$ETAG" \
  --query 'Distribution.{Status:Status}'

# After enabling, monitor OriginShield cache hit rate separately
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/CloudFront \
  --metric-name OriginShieldCacheHitRate \
  --dimensions Name=DistributionId,Value="$DIST_ID" Name=Region,Value=Global \
  --start-time "$(date -u -d '1 hour ago' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')" \
  --end-time "$(date -u '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')" \
  --period 300 --statistics Average --output table
Cache Behaviors: Per-Path TTL and Function Policies
A CloudFront distribution can have multiple
— each matches a path pattern and applies its own TTL, origin, viewer protocol policy, and Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Function associations. This lets you serve a single domain from multiple backends:
Lambda@Edge: image resize at origin request

# Add a cache behavior for /api/* with no caching and JWT auth via Lambda@Edge
# First, publish the Lambda@Edge function (must be in us-east-1)
FUNC_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jwt-verify:3"

CONFIG=$(aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id "$DIST_ID")
ETAG=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.ETag')

# Insert cache behavior for /api/*
echo "$CONFIG" | jq '
  .DistributionConfig.CacheBehaviors.Quantity += 1 |
  .DistributionConfig.CacheBehaviors.Items += [{
    "PathPattern": "/api/*",
    "TargetOriginId": "ALB-api",
    "ViewerProtocolPolicy": "https-only",
    "CachePolicyId": "4135ea2d-6df8-44a3-9df3-4b5a84be39ad",
    "LambdaFunctionAssociations": {
      "Quantity": 1,
      "Items": [{
        "LambdaFunctionARN": "'"$FUNC_ARN"'",
        "EventType": "viewer-request",
        "IncludeBody": false
      }]
    }
  }]
' | jq '.DistributionConfig' > /tmp/behaviors-config.json

aws cloudfront update-distribution \
  --id "$DIST_ID" \
  --distribution-config file:///tmp/behaviors-config.json \
  --if-match "$ETAG" --query 'Distribution.Status' --output text
, not general-purpose compute. They run CloudFront, Route 53, Shield, and WAF — not EC2 or RDS.
(PoP → Regional Edge Cache → AWS Region) ensures users get cached content from the closest possible tier, reducing origin load by 90%+ for cacheable content.
runs at PoPs with full Node.js/Python runtimes (up to 30 s);
run at PoPs with < 1 ms latency for simple header/URL manipulation.
let you restrict PoP coverage to control cost when global coverage is not needed.
for static assets via long TTLs and versioned URLs — invalidations are a recovery mechanism, not a deployment strategy.
